title: How to balance it all - Career lessons from a multitalented data consultant #techpeepsDiaspora

So I went to Alliance Française for 3 months after junior high school. I just had this crazy interest for the French language because my dad travels a lot, and every time he came back, he came back with some language CDs. So it just happened that I was always, you know, getting into the French language. I became so good at it, my professor even was so interested, like very excited by the way I was writing my essays. Now, I wanted to be a pilot. Some parts of me still want to be a pilot. You could become incapacitated, you may not be able to travel again, at least have a degree that you can fall on so that in case anything happens, you can still, you know, have a career. So at the time it sounded like good advice, you know, and tech was, you know, you could work everywhere. So I went forward to read ITI University of Ghana.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Hi everyone, and welcome to another episode of Tech Peeps Diaspora. On this show, we have conversations with successful tech professionals from Africa and the diaspora, such as software engineers, data analysts, product managers, and many more. So our guest for today is Raymond. Hi Raymond, and welcome to the show.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Thank you so much for having me, Zach. It's a pleasure to be here.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Uh, I was, you know, kind of hesitant to pronounce your full name because it's a really long name, but I'm gonna leave that to you. So could you, our audience, give us a brief introduction about yourself, who you are, and what you do?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Okay, so thank you so much for having me, Zach, once again. So my name is Raymond Ewin Amklutse. Um, I am a trained data scientist currently working as a data consultant. Here in the UK. I've been in my company BigSpark for about almost 3 years. I'm a musician. I am also a musician apart from my day, my work. So I do create music and I'm the only boy for my parents. I love to talk. I'm a sanguine, but I also have, you know, my days of where I like to also, you know, hide from everyone and go on my periodic hiatus. I like interacting with people. I like having conversations and I'm actually very glad to have this conversation with you.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Yeah, I'm looking forward to this. You know, before the call, I was kind of thinking to myself how this conversation is going to go. Because also, like, from your profile, I see that, like, you speak fluently multiple languages. You also have a podcast, right? Apart from you also cook. So, you know, I was kind of thinking where I want to take this conversation to, but I'll just let you be the captain of this ship.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Wherever you want to go, I'll go with you.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Yeah, but I'm not going to be too selfish. So I'm gonna try to stick to the tech stuff, right? I think for most of our viewers, like, something that would be really interesting is like, what does a data scientist or a data analyst do? Could you give us, you know, some explanation of what the role, um, is about?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Right, right. So essentially, a data analyst is looking out for, you know, some insights in the data. So a data analyst will have some questions, you know, in terms of, um, what that— what does the distribution look like? What, um, um, what, um, what profit have we— has the company been able to make over a period of time? What are some of the factors that contributed to that? So it's just a general insight into that. However, the work is gradually, you know, encapsulating, or it's actually including other areas where people are now going further to do some form of, you know, visualization and all that. So you can see there's a bit of, um, an overlap with BI and all that. But essentially, a data analyst's job is to be able to present data in an in an understandable format to stakeholders to understand or to be able to answer some questions that stakeholders have. For a data scientist, however, it's the same data, or it could be the same data that they have, but this time they want to be able to make some predictions. So the data boom, you know, people say data is the new oil. So everyone is, you know, getting into data whatsoever. So we have the analytics bit of it, and then we have the data science bit of it, where now you want to be able to see if you can identify some patterns in that data. So over a period of time, you can realize that, okay, these are different features that, um, are able to help you make some prediction about, for example, if, um, someone is male or female. I know for the context of those who are watching and listening, um, I'm going to put it in the boundaries of just male and female without necessarily going out of those boundaries. But say that is what you want to necessarily predict on, on a biological sense. You have a few features, whether it has to do with height whether it has to do with color, all those kind of stuff. A data scientist will want to see how I can gain some insight, identify if there's some specific patterns that cut across all males or all females to be able to make a prediction. Another way of, or another, that description can be, you know, made more clear is making a prediction about whether there's going to be an increase of maybe house prices over house prices in a particular area over a period of time. So what a data scientist will have is data over a period of time of the different prices, the different factors that affected it, whether it had to do with, you know, GDP, whether it had to do with even, you know, policy or whatsoever, and see if all those, you know, variables coming together are able to help us make predictions of maybe next year based on what we have learned over the period of time. We can make some predictions. So basically a data, a data analyst is looking forward to gain some firsthand insights into their data. Whilst a data scientist's work will be more about predicting the next event based on the data they currently have.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Yeah, that kind of gives me, you know, um, an impression that like these, um, roles are closely related and probably they work together, but we are gonna get, you know, a bit deep into, into that later. Mm-hmm. Um, now I'm kind of. Interested in what your background is like. What was it like growing up, you know, family, school, stuff like that?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: All right, okay. So I grew up in Ghana, and, um, so I went to junior high school, and then I went forward to do my senior high school at Infansfim, where I had the chance to be the domestic head prefect. It's, uh, it's something that I'd rarely say, but because I just came on board here, I'll just mention it. And there, um, before I even went to high school, I had a crazy interest for French. So I went to Alliance Française for 3 months after junior high school. I just had this crazy interest for the French language because my dad travels a lot, and every time he came back, he came back with some language CDs. So it just happened that I was always, you know, getting into the French language. I became so good at it, my professor even was so interested like very excited by the way I was writing my essays. But then I went to senior high school and I didn't, you know, really do anything with the French. So I read science with geography. I didn't really like biology because I didn't really think I could survive it. So fast forward, after 3 years, I was now getting ready to go and do a bachelor's degree. Now I wanted to be a pilot. Some parts of me still want to be a pilot. By the time my dad, who works in the industry, said planes are becoming computerized. So if you could, you know, up your skill, get to know a bit about this whole tech anything could happen. You could become incapacitated, you may not be able to travel again. At least have a degree that you can fall on so that in case anything happens, you can still, you know, have a career. So at the time it sounded like good advice, you know, and tech was, you know, you could work everywhere. So I went forward to read IT at University of Ghana. I don't think I entered University of Ghana being excited about learning or doing tech. I think I did it because I felt it was going to be a gateway for me to get into the the field I wanted to get into, that is, you know, being a pilot. So I struggled, and I say this, you know, confident that I did really struggle during my time in University of Ghana, because I look back at the time I spent there and I look at the things that I know now, and I feel that if maybe if I'd gone in there with a more— if I'd gone in there more informed, if I'd gone in there with more enthusiasm, I would have probably been able to, you know, make some money and maybe be, be a better developer than I am today. Um, so I didn't finish as well as I wanted to. Um, I did, I broke my back as much as I could. Um, I do not regret the journey because I believe that all adds up to where I am today. So I went, so I did my IT degree, graduated, and my final year project was on a traffic, um, navigation system. So at the time Google did not have any traffic information for Ghana, so knowing whether there's going to be traffic congestion here or there. So I saw the opportunity, and at that time I'd, you know, studied a bit of, you know, algorithms, and I got to know about the A* algorithm, Dijkstra's algorithm for pathways and all that. So I wanted to, you know, add all that and be able to do something with what I'd learned, because a lot of the things I was doing at a particular point in time was very, you know, hypothetical. It was very theoretical. We— I'll go into the class and we are doing coding stuff, and we are using Visual Basic or we are coding in Java whatsoever. It just felt so far away from me. I was not really, I wasn't really connecting as much, but it was my final year project that I felt like, okay, I was doing something that really touched or that really reflected the skills that I had. So in terms of the app, so I did it in Android, so I did use Android Studio and all that. In terms of the app, it wasn't really a good looking app because I'm not a UI/UX designer. Designer. No, it was a good app. But in terms of functionality, I really learned a lot. I got to know about the Google API, how to be able to detect speed. So there were times where I used to run in my house to be able to see if I could see this. And it was very fun times. And my sister and I talk about it a lot. She can't still believe that I am still in tech because she looks back at how, um, how I struggled, but then I still find myself here. So fast forward I didn't finish as a very top student in University of Ghana or in the IT class for different reasons, some of which I would not mention, some of which were even beyond us as students. But then, because my department really liked the projects I worked on, they wanted me to stay as a teaching assistant. And if anyone knows, you staying as a teaching assistant, it means that you are, you know, really brainy. You know, you are a first-class student who knows his way around, or his or her way around things. But that wasn't my case. Um, so I really struggled with the idea of, you know, staying as a teaching assistant. And guess what, at the same time too, I had the opportunity to go and work with the IT department at Starbull, um, one of the airlines at the time, you know, second to Africa World. And I'd done an internship there prior in their IT department, and at least it was a clear gateway. I want to be a pilot. So get to, you know, be around the pilots. And should an opportunity present itself, I just go to piloting school. Unfortunately, they collapsed during the time when I was getting ready. So a lot of things didn't work in my favor in terms of that national service. So my dad— so I had, I had both options, whether to go and do it at Starwood or to do it at University of Ghana. Spoke with my parents, spoke with my dad, a bit of consultation, and then I decided to be a teaching assistant. Which then opened me to an amazing man that I always talk about in my life, Mr. Anand, who was doing his professional doctorate in data science at University of East London. And he mentioned that whole aspect of data science. And I was like, what is this? Because I was looking for a way out. And yes, that's basically the summary of how I found myself in data science. If you want to know, I mean, you can go on and on how to— I eventually went to get a master's in data science, and I even find that to be very surreal. But yes, that's a bit of, you know, the summary of my tech journey. I hope I've been able to capture and answer the question clearly.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Yeah, actually, like, I'm really interested to, you know, go, um, deep down that route. Um, but one thing that you said, um, which really caught my attention was the fact that, you know, before you went to university and to study IT, you didn't have any prior, um, knowledge of what to expect and stuff like that. And I think that is you know, um, a similar experience that most people, at least like people that go to, um, school in Ghana also face, you know, for their courses. Like, what do you think? Um, this is usually the case.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Um, so for me, my sister was, um, in University of Mines and Technology, and she had this great set of guys who were the Glyvion guys, they were doing some amazing stuff, and it was good, and it was great. But I think I didn't take my time to get to know what they were doing. She just told me about the fancy stuff they were doing, but I didn't get the chance to interact with them one-on-one. And that's advice I'll give to anyone who is starting something new, whether it's a podcast, whether it's a— whether it's a business or whatsoever. Information is, is in abundance all over the internet. Don't just jump into something blindly thinking it's going to be all hunky-dory and rosy. If you're thinking about a career, you know, most schools have this kind of, you know, career development, or, you know, have these career fairs and everything. Some schools may not necessarily have it. Um, a lot of companies are also looking at ways to, you know, hire people for like internships. You know, even junior high school students get a chance to, you know, intern there. Those opportunities will give you the chance to be able to interact with people.. If there are bootcamps or if you're active on LinkedIn, just read, read and get to know what it is like on a daily, because there's a social media bit of the work that people aspire to do. I had a conversation with a lady and she was like, the glamour around being a lawyer is exciting, but the everyday work of being a lawyer, it's not very exciting. Reading cases, a lot of things, it's not exciting. We are both developers. People see it and all they just see is the money. Oh my God, you have a house, you have that kind of thing, you have two MacBook Pros and everything. That is great. But then the everyday work of a developer where you are sitting behind a bug, or it may be you're trying to, you know, install a software to do your actual coding. Like it happened to me two days ago, I was trying to install something with WSL in one of our client systems. It took me about two days to just even get that thing up and running because of the different proxies and everything. So just have conversations with people. If you aspire to be like people, sit with them and don't just ask them the, the glamour, or the, the glamorizing questions of, oh, so how much money do you make? No, ask them the question, what does it take to make money like this? What does it take to have a sustained career like that? So that when you are getting in there, you know that you're going towards a goal. And that's a mistake I believe I made. Although IT was not the final destination for me, if I had spent a little bit more time talking with these guys that my sister was mentioning, Glyvian and all that, maybe they could have been of better mentorship to even guide the, the, the work I did. But the funny thing is, life comes to you in a full circle because I didn't get the mentorship at the beginning, but I got it at the end. And I got a chance to work with them on a project that was similar to, you know, Uber. So we're trying to create, you know, an Uber-like system for, for clients in Canada. And that's where even the whole thing about, you know, traffic engine thing I was working on, seeing the speed and all that. I got to see Android development in a better light. I got to see my mistakes of when to use a fragment and when to use an activity. I got to be mentored by those amazing guys. So Saeed Thomas— oh, you know him? Yeah, I think he's— I think he's—

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: I think he's—

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: I think— yes, I think he's in Germany. So I got a chance to, you know, interact with Saeed, um, Thomas Darko, and Fitz Gerald, those guys, when I think about my tech journey, I really respect the patience they had with me cuz I wasn't a great developer. I don't even still think I'm too great a developer now, but I'm better than where I used to be, definitely.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: So, uh, fast forward. So you had like, uh, some sort of, you know, basis foundation after university, you know, after building your project, you learned a few stuff. Fast forward, you know, um, you did a master's. I'm sure like there are certain things that happened along the way which, you know, um, something must have given you the confidence to say, okay, now I'm ready to enter into like the, the job market. What was that process for you after school?

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Right, so, so doing like, um, having spent some time with my boss and he kept on talking about the whole data science thing, that was the first time I heard about data and science. It was all weird to me. Um, so I applied to a school, a top university in, um, in France. I didn't— I actually didn't even know they were a top university until, um, I got in and they gave me IoT. Now, if anyone knows me from my days in University of Ghana, I didn't like networking. Cisco Packet Tracer always gave me headache. I tried to understand DNS. I mean, I still do now. I, I, I understand it much better because I, I've gone further to, you know, expand my knowledge in the cloud and all that. But at the time, it wasn't really interesting for me. Although at the time, you know, people, you know, the whole world was— the whole world is going to be connected. IoT, you're going to see, um, this, uh, um, device connected here. So it was, you know, the hype, and it's always exciting. Um, the school I wanted to go to, the other school I wanted to go to, they didn't give me the data. It's like In fact, they didn't even admit me at all. So I spoke with my parents, they were like, you know what, just go and do this IOT and let's at least have it as a master's, and then later you can pivot. So I went to my school and the first— so the first year or the first semester, it was a French school. So I did my— actually my studies in both French and English. So it was— yes, it was a bit of a challenge even though I spoke a bit of French, and I'll come to that because I went to French summer school a month before. I love the French language, So for me it was like an opportunity to learn it more. Um, so I, um, so the first semester, or yeah, like, so the first semester they make all of us do the same thing. So those who have, those who are supposed to do data science, those who are supposed to do IoT, those who are supposed to do, um, information security, governance, whatever it is, we all do the same thing. And I did about 16 courses in the first semester. It was intense. If anyone knows about the French educational system is very, very rigorous. I was spending my entire day indoors. Um, I was only Ghanaian in my school because Ghanaians seldom go to France to go and do their course in French and English, let alone in a comp— in a, in a part of the country that was in the outskirts. I was in the, um, Western Atlantic, you know. It was away from the life of— I was in Paris, I was at Brest. And if you come out of my school, you're going to the ocean. So that was, that was the kind of life I was living, was very, you know, um, living in the, in the countryside, you know. And, um, it was a very, very hard experience for me because a part of me was not very happy with IoT that I wanted to do. But just seeing that first semester and the rigor involved, it was very challenging. So we had a meeting with one of the, the core supervisors, and he said he knows a few people want to change their courses, but then they are only going to do that based on our performance in the first semester. So I went further to, you know, break my back. So I was spending time every single day in the library, group work. It was, it was a lot of rigor. It was a lot. In fact, I have had, I have had challenges in my life, and I even thought, I think some of the things I've had in my life— some of the events that have transpired in my life I thought were very painful. Every now and then when I think back at the kind of rigor I had, I was like, you know what, maybe what I'm going through is not as bad. But, um, they required us to, you know, pass our statistics, our math, our programming for them to consider you for another, um, for another, uh, course. And for the data science course, they were looking for, you know, C and Python and all that. It was a very challenging time because I had passed some of the courses that they were considering, and now I put in my application for a change of course, only for me to put in the application and then some of the courses that is required I failed. But fortunately for me, the fail didn't reflect right away, so they had already accepted me into the data science program. But then just when I'm now moving into a new domain, because I hadn't done so much statistics, or like I didn't, I didn't do a lot of statistics back in university and all that, I still had to write or rewrite, like reset 2 modules in that same semester together with my data science program. It was so hard. And that semester was going to be the semester we were going to study in French. We're talking about presentations, we're talking about meeting clients, we're talking about doing, um, a French project, I had a chance to work with some guys on using 3D modeling of bio-friendly reeds of a bassoon to be able to see if we can create— because the reed is mostly made of wood, all right? It's made of wood. But they wanted to see if we could have a bio-friendly 3D version that can produce the same sound waves, so we're doing, you know, signal processing and Fourier transform. It was very intense and all of that in French. I was the only Ghanaian and the Black person in the team. And I keep saying that the kind of rig I went through in France was such a challenging one, but it really built me in so many ways and I'm really grateful for that. So I ended up getting into the data science program, was a chance to work with— so it was a class of like about 7 to 8 people, and one guy even dropped out at a point because it was too intense. During that period, we studied, you know, big data, data science, so modeling, did a bit of, you know, um, cloud. There was a lot, there was a lot. We, you know, interacted with clients, market, customer segmentation, prediction. It was a lot of, you know, it was a lot of rigor. And then the And then the last semester was preparing you towards that period to go into market. So my school, so in, I don't know if how it is in Germany, but in my school you're required to do, um, a 6-month intensive internship at the end of your program. I wanted to get more experience. So after my first semester, I got a chance to work in my school's incubator as an AI engineer. So I was working on, you know, some sentiment analysis using pre-trained models to be able be able to detect. So we're doing text, so doing speech to text, and we're supposed to do it on French. So the pre-trained model was trained on English, but then it can be able to make predictions in French. So I had to now use that, put it together, it was a lot. And then I had a chance to work in a fintech, um, at the end of my, my program. So they were working on, you know, credit scoring models to be able to predict, to, to be able to predict if people are you know, high risk or not. And that's where I got a chance to do low-level, dirty, um, data science work. And yeah, so that preparation for industry— my school put a structure together that prepared you for that. Then COVID happened, and, um, we'll talk about that if you want to get to know about the details of that. But yes, um, yeah, that was pretty much the journey of preparing me to be ready for industry.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Right. So, and you know, now you are, um, a data scientist and data analyst at Spark. BigSpark. BigSpark. Like what's your, you know, day to day? And even before we get to that, like I'm curious how, you know, like the interview process for like your role is like, um, could you like give example of how it was? At least for, you know, you know, getting into Big Spark.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: I'm a bit of a storyteller, so I'll pull you back into the COVID and bring you back in. So during COVID you know, after school COVID happened, um, I was looking for a job. So I was at home for almost close to a year, had to go back to Ghana, come back again. And a friend of mine was also looking for a job at the same time, and he mentioned that his company is looking for, um— he got a job and he told me his company is looking for data engineers. He asked me a few questions about Spark, Hadoop, the typical stack. And I was like, oh yeah, I know this stuff. I've been able to do some projects whilst I was in school. He recommended me. So I got my job through recommendation. So he recommended me. The boss checked me out, saw my skills. He was like, okay, I like this guy. He's very determined, blah, blah, blah. So it was first an informal discussion with the boss. They just get to know who you are, get to know how you behave and everything. Then I was given a technical challenge. So I was given a data engineering challenge to, you know, tackle. So it was a take-home challenge. I had to get back to them like a week. They gave me all the systems that I had to work with. I had to do use Zeppelin. You had to set up the entire environment and all. The beautiful thing is at the time I had a chance to be able to interact with my boss because it was a startup. They were getting ready to get a lot more people in. So that was, that was how it went. I did a technical challenge. I had to now prepare for the presentation, so I went further to do a PPT, presented my findings. So I showed them the code. I pushed my code to GitHub for them to get to know that I know, you know, how to work with, um, like, um, exactly, source, like source control and like all that. So I was very keen on, you know, showing the different skills that will, you know, make me stand out. And I spoke about that a lot. Then I, after I went into the the, the, the details of the work, how I cleaned up the data, all that kind of stuff, the pipelining. And then I showed the work. So it was a 3-stage interview. Then after I had the final conversation with the boss, guess, just get to know me on a human level. What am I like? What are my strengths? Am I more technical than managerial? What do I see myself doing in the long term? And then they liked me. So then they sponsored my visa for me to move from France to the UK. So my trajectory was a rather an was a rather interesting one. Not everyone gets a chance to come and study in one other country and then they get a chance to be poached into another country. I wouldn't say I'm the smartest in the tech space. I, I really give all the credit to God. I'm very much Christian, so I give all the credit to God for opening that door for me. And, um, yeah, so that's how I got into BigSpark, and I've been there for almost— this May will be 3 years since I joined BigSpark.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Yeah, nice. That's really interesting. So what's, uh, like your day-to-day, like working at BigSpark, what's the vibe like working in the teams, what kind of products you're working on.

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: All right, so you did say I work as a data analyst and data scientist. I just want to correct that a little bit. I am a trained data scientist, but I'm currently working as a data consultant. So I'm working as a consulting firm, and then we consult for, um, a variety of clients, more, most of which are financial services. Services. So currently working with one of like the teams where we consult for banks. I'm working in the, in the team that is doing a project with the bank. Um, so the thing about consulting is you are wearing so many hats, you know, you are doing what the client wants. So even though I'm a trained data scientist, I'll be lying to you if I said I have done a lot of data science work since I started at BigSpark. I've done more data engineering, more software engineering, and DevOps. So my first work at back was to be able to create, um, it was more software engineering focused. I was supposed to create a connector for one of our data engineering platforms, which is StreamSets. So we had, I had to create a connector that was gonna connect to, um, Neo4j. So they, they, they didn't have a, they didn't have a Neo4j connector. So I had to now create a custom one. So I had to go into the Neo4j website, get to see the API, get to see all what all that looks like, wrap that around. Some Python code, or it was actually Java, sorry. I will wrap that around Java and then go through the whole creating the JAR and then uploading that to the StreamSets platform. That was my first task, and I had the chance to be able to see a pipeline from end to end and what that looks like. And then when I, when I went to the client side, um, it was more of— at the beginning, it was more of, um, banks sometimes maybe using a bit of Excel here and there. So it was more of, you know, working around some of those systems until I was put on the task to develop— it was a Python package to be able to do some data engineering stuff. So it was— they had a feature store and then they wanted to be able to read those features in a seeming— like, like in a, in a seamless manner. So I had to now go through, you know, using different, you know, calling functions upon functions and all that kind of stuff. So I had to implement a lot of things to be able to create that system, and which gladly is currently still being used in the, in the bank. So that was the project I worked on. So the beginning was very much software engineering focus and a bit of data engineering. So that particular task, so we had the data stored in a data store, so it was in Hadoop, and then I had to now create that Python package to be able to read it. And the goal is to read it in in an efficient manner. Because when you are all— because if we are giving people the chance to write their own scripts to read data, they may not have very optimized, you know, data pipelines and all that. And then it could, you know, affect the system and cause a lag. So the goal was to have one point of entry into that data warehouse or into that data lake. It's a data lake we actually had so that we are the ones, like, we are the ones on our layer are able to control how that data is queried and how that data is manipulated and presented back to the user. Um, I did that for a while until I was moved to another team where we're, where we're developing an API and how to use GraphQL, Spring Boot. So I did a bit more of, you know, API development stuff. Um, and then currently I'm now doing more of DevOps, um, GitLab, here and there. So consulting is very, you know, you have to meet the demands of the client. You go in there, you may have interests apart from doing that on the consulting side. I also have my own thing that I've been doing internally in BigSpark, trying to work on the transpiler. So using ASTs, and I know it sounds now, I know I've mentioned a lot of terms and people, my, some of my friends will probably be watching this video and be like, oh my God, wow, Raymond sounds very brainy. It's been a long journey. Even, even for me, talking about these things on a, on, on a podcast, on a YouTube channel, um, makes me appreciate the growth. Although sometimes I feel like, okay, maybe I've not made as much growth, uh, makes me appreciate the growth. So how does it feel like to work in BigSpark? So BigSpark is a multicultural company. Currently we have about 18, or 15 to 18 Ghanaians in the— if I'm mistaken. Yeah, so it's quite a small Ghanaian community It's a fully remote company, so you can work from anywhere, but then you have to be in the UK because of our clients' requests. Um, but then we have different silos, so we have people in Leeds, people in London area, people in Scotland. So they do have their meetups from time to time online. We try to, you know, engage on different— like, in, in different activities, you know, keep the vibe going. We have, um, periodic meetups, so the people in London area, the people in the Midlands, the people. In, um, in, in, in Scotland, they do get to meet, interact, and we do have, uh, our yearly, um, company meetings, which one will be coming up very soon in April where we meet up, we chit-chat, those who need to be congratulated for blah, blah, blah. So we are very much a remote company. So it's more of a lot, a lot of things are done online. If need be that we meet face to to face. Yes, when the need arises, we do get sensitive.

segments.speaker: HOST

segments.speaker: GUEST
segments.text: Um, I would say be patient with yourself. In this journey. You know, I realized that we tend to be very hard on ourselves when things don't go as planned, and I'm a culprit. Look at me, I didn't really see myself, you know, doing tech, but now I'm here going to start working with the university on a tech project. So celebrate the small wins, um, take inventory of how far you've come. Try and just speak with people who are ahead of you. Get mentors if you can. If you cannot get mentors, become a mentor, because by mentoring you start seeing a few gaps and then you see what you could have done better. Then you are able to tailor your learning further. Be curious. Do not be afraid of new environment. I went to France, do my master's. I did my master's in French and in English. Had a winding road with that. It wasn't comfortable, but that has built me. And my personal mantra is to stay resilient and to go for gold, and I've had to live that mantra in so many ways altogether. So stay curious, allow yourself to breathe. If you need to cry sometimes, don't be afraid to cry. It's very normal. Human beings, whether you're a man or woman, cry if you need to, but don't sweat the small stuff. In the grand scheme of things, you look back and you realize that you, you, you look back and you realize that you may, you may have robbed yourself of some opportunities because you were thinking too much about how incapable you are. We all have our strengths and weaknesses. Try and see how you can expand your strengths or use your strengths more, and, and also then see how you can translate your weaknesses into more of strength. And the ones that you cannot do much about about. You take it as is and you just look to the future for more, um, more opportunities. And then yeah, stay active as well, um, network as much as you can. You know, online presence is very important. Don't be afraid to put yourself out there.
